1: eleh hachuqqim vehamishpatim (ʾēlleh haḥuqqîm wĕhammišpāṭîm): 'These are the statutes and ordinances.' Chapter 12 opens the Deuteronomic Law Code (chs 12-26) — the specific legal applications of the broad covenant command of chs 1-11. ḥuqqîm (statutes, fixed rules) and mišpāṭîm (ordinances, case-law decisions): the two principal legal categories. The land is the context — 'in the land which YHWH the God of your fathers has given you to possess, all the days that you live on the earth.'
2: abbed tabdun et-kol-hamqomot (ʾabbēd tĕʾabbĕdûn ʾet-kol-hammĕqōmôt): 'Completely destroy all the places.' Again the intensive double construction (ʾabbēd tĕʾabbĕdûn — double piel of ʾābad, to destroy). mĕqōmôt (places): the high places, hillsides, and trees where the Canaanite gods were worshipped. The command is not merely to stop using them but to eliminate them — ʿal-hehārîm hārāmîm wĕʿal-haggĕbāʿôt wĕtaḥat kol-ʿēṣ raʿănān (on the high mountains, on the hills, under every green tree).
3: nittazttem et-mizbehotam: 'Tear down their altars.' The four verbs of cultic destruction: nāttaṣ (tear down), šābar (smash), śārap̄ (burn), kārat (cut down). Each object: mizbĕḥôt (altars), maṣṣēbôt (sacred pillars, standing stones), ʾăšērîm (Asherah poles, wooden cult objects), and pĕsîlîm (carved/graven images). The divine name (šēm) of the Canaanite deities is to be destroyed — the name represents the deity's presence and power.
4: lo-taaʿsun ken laYHWH eloheichem: 'You must not do so to YHWH your God.' The explicit prohibition of Canaanite worship-practices applied to YHWH — YHWH is not to be worshipped in the same manner as the Canaanite gods. The verse is cryptic but decisive: whatever the Canaanites did at their scattered high places is forbidden for YHWH's worship. The positive alternative follows in v.5: YHWH chooses a single place.
5: ki im el-hamaqom asher-yivchar YHWH eloheichem (kî ʾim-ʾel-hammāqôm ʾăšer-yibḥar Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem): 'Only to the place that YHWH your God chooses.' The theological heart of Deuteronomy 12: the centralization formula. yibḥar (he will choose): the divine election of the sanctuary site is YHWH's prerogative, not Israel's. lĕšakkēn šĕmô šām (to make his name dwell there): the Name-theology of Deuteronomy — YHWH does not corporally dwell in the temple (contrast Solomon's prayer, 1 Kings 8:27: 'even heaven cannot contain you') but places his šēm (name, representative presence) there. This nuance safeguards divine transcendence while asserting real divine presence.
6: uveatem shama oloteichem: 'There you shall bring your burnt offerings.' The list of sacrificial types required at the central sanctuary: ʿōlôt (burnt offerings), zeḇāḥîm (sacrifices), maʿăśrôt (tithes), tĕrûmat yēdkem (heave offerings), nĕdārîm (vow offerings), nĕdābôt (freewill offerings), and the firstborn of flocks and herds. The comprehensive catalog emphasizes that all worship-transactions with YHWH require the central place.
7: vaachaltem sham lifnei YHWH eloheichem: 'There you shall eat before YHWH your God.' The covenant meal eaten 'before YHWH' (lip̄nê Yhwh) in the sanctuary is not merely a meal but a communion act — Israel eating in the divine presence. ûśĕmaḥtem (rejoice): the joy-command that runs through Deuteronomy's festival legislation (14:26; 16:11,14; 26:11). The centralized sanctuary is not an onerous restriction but the place of covenant celebration and abundance.
8: lo taaʿsun kekol asher anachnu osim po hayom: 'You shall not do as we are doing here today.' The ʾîš kol-hayyāšār bĕʿênāyw (each man doing what is right in his own eyes) formula — the antithesis of the centralized covenant order. The chaotic worship of the wilderness period (each according to his own judgment) is contrasted with the ordered central-sanctuary worship that the entry into the land will establish. Judges 17:6; 21:25 use the same phrase for the moral chaos of the settlement period.
9: ki lo batem ad-ata el-hamenucha veEl-hanachala: 'You have not yet come to the rest and the inheritance.' mĕnûḥâ (rest, resting-place): the theological rest that the land represents — the cessation of the wilderness wandering. naḥălâ (inheritance): the land as divine gift-inheritance, not merely a territory won by military conquest. The double term anticipates the settled, stable worship at the chosen place — rest and inheritance are prerequisites for the centralized sanctuary order.
10: vaavartm et-haYarden vishavtem baareẓ: 'When you cross the Jordan and dwell in the land.' The conditional structure of Deut 12:1-28 — the laws apply fully only when the land is possessed and rest is achieved. wĕhēnîaḥ lākem mibbāl ʾōyĕbêkem (when he gives you rest from all your enemies): the military rest is the precondition for the cultic order. Hebrews 4:1-11 uses the mĕnûḥâ concept as a type for the final eschatological rest in Christ.
11: vehaya hamaqom asher-yivchar YHWH eloheichem bo: 'It shall be the place YHWH your God chooses for his name to dwell.' The centralization formula repeated (vv.5,11,14,18,21,26) — its ninefold repetition in ch 12 marks it as the theological spine of the entire legal corpus. The accumulated weight of 'the place that YHWH chooses' drives home the principle: divine election, not human convenience, determines the locus of worship.
12: usmachtem lifnei YHWH eloheichem: 'Rejoice before YHWH your God.' śāmaḥ (to rejoice) is a covenant-obligation as much as a response. The inclusion of sons, daughters, male and female servants, and the Levite within your gates — the total household including dependents — in the sanctuary celebration is Deuteronomy's consistent social vision: covenant joy is communal and inclusive.
13: hishamer lecha pen-taaleh oloteicha: 'Take care not to offer your burnt offerings at every place.' The negative form of the centralization command — explicit prohibition of sacrifice at any location chosen by the worshipper. hišāmer (take care, be careful): the reflexive warning. The positive follows: 'only in the place that YHWH chooses in one of your tribes.'
14: ki im-bamaqom asher-yivchar YHWH: 'Only in the place that YHWH chooses in one of your tribes.' The indefiniteness of 'one of your tribes' is deliberate — the specific place (Jerusalem/Zion) is not named in Deuteronomy. The principle of divine election precedes the specific location; the people commit to following YHWH's choice before the choice is made known.
15: raq bechol-avat nafshecha tizbach veachalta vasar: 'Only you may slaughter and eat meat within your towns whenever you desire.' The concession for secular slaughter (mĕhôl) outside the sanctuary — meat may be eaten for sustenance without sacrificial ritual, unlike the earlier system (Lev 17:1-7 required all slaughter to be sacrificial). Deuteronomy permits what is called šĕḥîṭat ḥullîn (secular/non-sacred slaughter) — a practical concession for the increased distance from the central sanctuary. The clean and unclean together may eat: haṭṭāhôr wĕhaṭṭāmēʾ — without the purity constraints of sacrificial eating.
16: raq hadam lo tochal: 'Only you shall not eat the blood.' dām (blood): the prohibition of blood-consumption is foundational (Lev 17:10-14; Gen 9:4; Acts 15:29). The blood belongs to YHWH as the vehicle of life — ʿal-hāʾāreṣ tišpĕkennû kammāyim (pour it on the ground like water): the blood is returned to the earth rather than consumed, acknowledging that the life belongs to God.
17: lo tuchal leechol bisheareicha maʿsar: 'You may not eat within your towns the tithe of your grain, wine, and oil.' The distinction between ordinary sustenance (permitted locally, v.15) and the covenant-sacred items (tithes, firstborn, vow offerings) that must be eaten at the central sanctuary. The list of items requiring pilgrimage to the central place: maʿăśar dĕgānĕkā wĕtîrōšĕkā wĕyiṣhārekā (tithes of grain, new wine, oil), firstborn of herd and flock, vow offerings, freewill offerings, heave offerings.
18: ki im-lifnei YHWH eloheicha tocharlennu: 'Only before YHWH your God shall you eat it.' The items withheld from local consumption must be consumed 'before YHWH' — in his presence, at the central sanctuary, in a communal celebration that includes the household and the Levite. The joy-command returns: ûśāmaḥtā lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā — the centralization's purpose is not restriction but celebration in the divine presence.
19: hishamer lecha pen-taazov et-halevi: 'Take care not to neglect the Levite.' The Levite has no territorial inheritance (ḥēleq wĕnaḥălâ) — his portion is YHWH himself (10:9; 18:1-2). The repeated concern for the Levite within Israel's towns across Deuteronomy (12:12,18,19; 14:27,29; 16:11,14; 26:12-13) reflects the social realities of cultic centralization: Levites who served at local shrines needed sustained community support once those shrines were abolished.
20: ki-yarchiv YHWH eloheicha et-gevulcha: 'When YHWH your God extends your territory.' The provision for secular meat-eating is made in anticipation of the land's expansion, when distance from the central sanctuary makes pilgrimage for every meat-meal impractical. weʾāmartā ʾōklâ bāśār (you say 'I will eat meat'): the natural human desire for meat is legitimate; YHWH's provision is practical and human-scale.
21: veHAYA im-yirchaq mimcha haMaqom asher yivchar YHWH: 'If the place that YHWH chooses is far from you.' Distance is the practical consideration that triggers the secular-slaughter permission. wĕzābaḥtā mibbĕqārĕkā ûmiṣṣōʾnĕkā (slaughter from your herd and flock) 'as I have commanded you' — even secular slaughter has a prescribed manner (ʾăšer ṣiwwîtîkā). The humane slaughter laws implicit in this phrase became the basis for rabbinic šĕḥîṭâ regulations.
22: ach kaaher yochal et-hatzvi veEt-haAYAL: 'Just as the gazelle and the deer are eaten.' The clean wild animals (whose blood is not brought to the altar and poured against it) become the model for secular slaughter — neither the blood-sprinkling ritual nor the fat-burning applies. The democratization of meat-eating that Deuteronomy permits is a significant departure from the Leviticus 17 system, reflecting the changed circumstances of settled land life far from the central sanctuary.
23: raq chazaq levilti achal hadam (raq ḥăzaq lĕbiltî ʾăkōl haddām): 'Only be strong in not eating the blood.' ḥăzaq (be strong, be firm): the blood prohibition requires moral strength and resolution to maintain. The theological rationale: kî haddām hûʾ hannep̄eš (for the blood is the life/soul). nepeš (life, soul, being): the blood as the carrier of the nepeš (the animating life-force) is the most fundamental principle of Israelite food law. Lev 17:11 adds: 'the life of the flesh is in the blood; I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your souls.'
24: lo tochlenu: 'Do not eat it.' The repeated blood prohibition (vv.16,23,24,25) through intensification. ʿal-hāʾāreṣ tišpĕkennû kammāyim (pour it on the ground like water): the blood returned to the earth reverses the sacrificial blood that is collected and dashed against the altar. Non-sacrificial slaughter still requires the blood's return — the life belongs to YHWH even when the killing is secular.
25: lo tochlenu lemaʿan yitav lecha: 'Do not eat it, that it may go well with you.' The motivational formula: obedience to the blood law produces well-being (ṭôb) for the individual and descendants. The covenant-reward structure: right practice → divine blessing. The blood law is not arbitrary but carries the weight of the covenant's own survival logic — the life belongs to the giver of life.
26: raq qodasheicha asher-yihyu lecha: 'But your holy things and your vow offerings.' The return to the central-sanctuary requirement for the sacred items — vowed sacrifices (nĕdārîm) must be brought to the chosen place. The distinction sacred/secular (consecrated offering vs. ordinary meat) determines where the eating occurs.
27: veAsita olotecha habasar vehadam: 'Offer your burnt offerings, the flesh and the blood, on the altar.' The burnt offering is the total offering — bāśār wĕdām (flesh and blood) both belong to YHWH, consumed entirely. The peace offering (zeḇaḥ šĕlāmîm) differs: the blood is dashed against the altar, the fat burned, but the flesh eaten by the worshipper 'before YHWH.' The two-way flow of the communion meal: YHWH receives the blood and fat, Israel eats the flesh.
28: shemor veshimaʿta et-kol-hadevarim haeleh: 'Observe and obey all these words.' The summary command closing the central-sanctuary legislation. lĕmaʿan yîṭab lĕkā ûlĕbānêkā (that it may go well for you and your children): the covenant-benefit formula applied to the entire legal corpus of ch 12. The chapter ends with the same well-being motivation as v.25 — the laws are for Israel's flourishing, not arbitrary restriction.
29: ki-yachrit YHWH eloheicha et-hagoyim: 'When YHWH your God cuts off the nations before you.' The dispossession formula opens the warning against Canaanite worship-imitation. pen-tinaqesh achareihem (lest you be ensnared by following them): nāqaš (to be ensnared, trapped): the Canaanite practices as a trap. The theological danger: Israel, having displaced the Canaanites, might be attracted to the cultic practices that 'worked' in the land.
30: pen-tidrosh leEloheihem leymor: 'Lest you inquire about their gods, saying.' The curiosity-trap — not active worship but theological inquiry into Canaanite religion. êkâ yaʿabdû haggôyîm hāʾēlleh ʾet-ʾĕlōhêhem (how did these nations serve their gods?): the inquiry that leads to syncretism. The imitative impulse is the gateway to apostasy — the Deuteronomist understands the psychological seduction of religious curiosity.
31: lo-taaʿseh ken lYHWH eloheicha: 'You shall not do so to YHWH your God.' The Canaanite worship practices are described as abominations (tôʿēbat Yhwh) — including child sacrifice (ki gam ʾet-bĕnêhem wĕʾet-bĕnōtêhem yiśrĕpû bāʾēš lēʾlōhêhem, 'for they even burned their sons and daughters in the fire for their gods'). The Molech cult is the extreme point of Canaanite religious practice — the total sacrifice of children to secure divine favor.
32: et-kol-haDavar asher anochi metzaveh etchem oto tishmerunu laasot: 'Everything I command you, be careful to do it.' The closing formula of chapter 12: neither add nor subtract (lōʾ tōsēp̄ ʿālāyw wĕlōʾ tigdaʿ mimmennû). The completeness and sufficiency of the Mosaic Torah — no human additions or subtractions permitted. Deut 4:2 opens with the same formula; the book is bracketed by the sufficiency of the divine word.
